evidence_quotes.quote: Nintendo released the videogame Super Mario Maker (SMM) in 2015 for its 3DS and Wii-U consoles. The game allowed players to create their own courses to play locally or upload them to the internet for other users to download and play.
The remaining online services for both the Wii U and 3DS versions of the game, such as playing levels uploaded before the portal site was discontinued, were shut down on April 8, 2024.
Nintendo's modern cartridges, for 3DS and Switch, apparently have a hardware "defect" that requires them to be used semi-regularly or risk becoming completely unusable.

paragraphs.text: Please consider the deletion of videogames. Here is a short case study/summary. From historyassociates Margaret Long: "Nintendo released the videogame Super Mario Maker (SMM) in 2015 for its 3DS and Wii-U consoles. The game allowed players to create their own courses to play locally or upload them to the internet for other users to download and play. The typical Mario game has 77 levels or more. SMM, on the other hand, saw the creation of over 7.2 million courses over the span of the games lifetime. It would take a single player more than 14 years to play all the levels in the game, Nintendo announced from its official Twitter account." From Wikipedia: "Sale of Super Mario Maker on the Wii U's Nintendo eShop was discontinued on January 12, 2021, and course upload support and the bookmark portal site was discontinued on March 31, 2021. The remaining online services for both the Wii U and 3DS versions of the game, such as playing levels uploaded before the portal site was discontinued, were shut down on April 8, 2024." Conclusion: Nintendo claimed a customer would need 14 years to play the levels created by the community of customers. But they removed access to the levels after 9 years. So Nintendo did not allow enough time for players to play all the levels. Nor did they make the levels (created by the community, for the community) available once they decided to shutdown the servers. In addition, Nintendo's modern cartridges, for 3DS and Switch, apparently have a hardware "defect" that requires them to be used semi-regularly or risk becoming completely unusable. Which would also affect the second hand market.
